name: Windows T1547 Detection via Run/RunOnce Registry Correlation

action--100c05a7-63e8-45c2-a40c-e51dc44e.name: Configure Sysmon (if available) with a configuration that includes logging of registry events for Run and RunOnce keys (e.g., using the Sysmon configuration from SwiftOnSecurity or Olaf Hartong).

action--1c7cdbca-4ce3-404d-97d7-ba4f3390.name: Establish a baseline of normal execution times for processes launched at logon/startup. Use statistical analysis or machine learning to identify outliers (e.g., processes executing at unusual times relative to user logon or system boot).

commands.command: Establish a baseline of normal execution times for processes launched at logon/startup. Use statistical analysis or machine learning to identify outliers (e.g., processes executing at unusual times relative to user logon or system boot).

action--21211198-05ed-46ed-b7df-f8a83aeb.name: Create alerts that trigger when a registry modification to a Run/RunOnce key is followed by the execution of a process with an abnormal parent-child relationship (e.g., a process spawned by an unknown or suspicious parent) and that occurs at an anomalous time during logon/startup.

action--7f8fe539-dd43-41e8-907f-1720f729.name: Correlate registry modification events with process creation events (Event ID 4688 or Sysmon Event ID 1) to establish parent-child process relationships. Focus on processes that are not typical parents for Run/RunOnce modifications (e.g., not explorer.exe, userinit.exe, or common installers).

description: This is an automatically generated playbook based on the question: A detections playbook for T1547 of MITRE ATT&CK Matrix for Enterprise framework is required.The target platform is Windows. You can find more information at https://attack.mitre.org/detectionstrategies/DET0274#AN0764 . The description of the approach follows: Correlation of registry key modification for Run/RunOnce with abnormal parent-child process relationships and outlier execution at user logon or system startup

external_references.name: MITRE ATT&CK Technique T1547: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution

items.content: Deploy and configure a SIEM or log aggregation tool to collect Windows Event Logs (specifically Security, System, and Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational if available).
Enable auditing for registry modifications. Specifically, enable 'Audit registry' in Advanced Audit Policy Configuration under Object Access.
Configure Sysmon (if available) with a configuration that includes logging of registry events for Run and RunOnce keys (e.g., using the Sysmon configuration from SwiftOnSecurity or Olaf Hartong).

Create detection rules to identify modifications to the following registry keys: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run, HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce, HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run, HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce.

Correlate registry modification events with process creation events (Event ID 4688 or Sysmon Event ID 1) to establish parent-child process relationships. Focus on processes that are not typical parents for Run/RunOnce modifications (e.g., not explorer.exe, userinit.exe, or common installers).

Establish a baseline of normal execution times for processes launched at logon/startup. Use statistical analysis or machine learning to identify outliers (e.g., processes executing at unusual times relative to user logon or system boot).

Create alerts that trigger when a registry modification to a Run/RunOnce key is followed by the execution of a process with an abnormal parent-child relationship (e.g., a process spawned by an unknown or suspicious parent) and that occurs at an anomalous time during logon/startup.
Investigate alerts by reviewing the full process tree, user context, command-line arguments, and file metadata (e.g., digital signatures) of the involved processes.

If malicious activity is confirmed, isolate the affected endpoint, collect forensic artifacts (e.g., memory dump, registry hives, and disk image), and eradicate the threat by removing persistence mechanisms and malicious files.
Update detection rules and baselines based on findings to improve future detection capabilities.
